storm.summary_layperson: No storm risk tonight — the radar is clear with no thunderstorms within range, and the National Weather Service's severe weather concern is for Tuesday afternoon and evening, not tonight.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The 71°F Sound water is 8°F cooler than the inland air, creating a moderate marine layer that would slow any approaching storms — but none are headed this way tonight.
storm.detail_html:
The radar is clean tonight — KOKX (Upton, NY) showed no storm cells or reflectivity above the detection threshold at its last scan (4:21 PM EDT). There are no active NWS alerts for the area, no upstream stations reporting storm-driven gusts, and the HRRR shows surface CAPE at 0 J/kg — essentially zero atmospheric instability. Tonight's 17:00–22:00 window carries no storm risk.

The National Weather Service Area Forecast Discussion (issued 4:03 PM EDT) does describe a significant severe weather and flash flood event — but for Tuesday, July 21, not tonight. The NWS cites "multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms Tuesday into Tuesday night" and an "enhanced risk of severe thunderstorms" for portions of the region during Tuesday's late afternoon and evening. An isolated strong storm is also possible Wednesday. Tonight sits ahead of that system; conditions will deteriorate tomorrow.

The moderate marine layer over Long Island Sound (Sound water 71°F, inland air 79°F, 8°F gap) provides natural resistance against convection — cool water over the Sound tends to weaken storm updrafts that try to cross it. The full cove-protection mechanism requires a 10°F differential to trigger, and tonight's 8°F gap falls slightly short. This context will be more relevant when evaluating tomorrow's storm approach; for tonight there is nothing for it to deflect.

Bulk wind shear is elevated at 29–31 kt (WNW, 0–6 km layer), which is an ingredient for organized convection. That shear combined with the approaching trough explains the NWS severe weather concern for Tuesday. For tonight's window, CAPE is zero, radar is clear, and there is nothing for the shear to organize. Keep an eye on the forecast Tuesday morning before heading out on the water.

wind.regime_label: Moderate gradient wind — no sea breeze in play
wind.tactics_blurb: In this southeast gradient, Greenwich Point shelters the east shore — the Belle Haven and Indian Harbor side carries fuller, steadier pressure while the east shore near Tod's Point runs lighter and shiftier. Start left and work the left lane upwind; expect a persistent, non-oscillating direction that slowly veers from SE toward SSE through the evening, with no wind loss after sunset.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a moderate gradient wind evening — the southerly flow is synoptic (pre-frontal, ahead of Tuesday's approaching trough) rather than thermally driven by a sea breeze. The sea-breeze test explains the call: while the air–water temperature difference of 8.1°F (inland air 79°F vs. Sound water 71°F) meets the 5°F thermal-potential threshold, only 48% of coastal stations are reporting southerly flow — short of the 60% needed to confirm sea-breeze activity. Both coastal and inland stations show similar southerly directions, the signature of gradient flow filling the full air column rather than a shallow sea breeze overriding a calm inland surface. The pipeline's sea-breeze flag is off, confirming the gradient-only regime.

Speed through the window: RYC WeatherLink shows SE 5 kt, 10-minute average 4.9 kt, gusting 9 kt at the cove right now (4:29 PM). HRRR forecasts a building trend — 6–7 kt for 17:00–18:00, rising to 8–9 kt for 19:00–21:00 as the pre-frontal gradient tightens — then holding near 8 kt at 22:00. No sunset collapse: gradient winds don't die with the sun (there is no thermal driver to lose), so expect the evening to hold through 22:00, softening only 1–2 kt after dark as the nocturnal boundary layer stabilizes. This is a solid 6–9 kt evening for most one-design classes; no postponement risk.

Direction: SE now (~145°), slowly veering to SSE (~158–162°) through the evening. One model/obs divergence worth noting: HRRR shows ESE (114–118°) for the 17:00–18:00 hours while current stations are already at SE–SSE (RYC 145°, western Long Island Sound zone median 156°). In a gradient-wind regime, observed station data better captures the current state, so the early window uses SE (~145°) from RYC as the working direction. HRRR's own forecast shifts to SSE (~158–162°) by 19:00, converging with current obs. Ensemble direction agreement is 0.66 — moderate spread, consistent with light pre-frontal mixing. The slow veer from SE to SSE is favorable: SSE is closer to the clean S axis, progressively reducing the Greenwich Point shadow effect as the evening progresses.

The cove geometry favors the left (west) side of the course in SE–SSE wind. Greenwich Point (Tod's Point) provides partial shelter on the east shore: boats along that side find lighter, more variable conditions in the point's wind shadow. The Belle Haven, Indian Harbor, and Field Point shore — left of the upwind axis looking toward SE — is clear of the shadow and carries fuller, steadier pressure. Start on the left side of the line, work upwind in the left lane, and be conservative on the starboard layline as pressure can be soft near Tod's Point on the right.

Shift character: persistent, not oscillating. Gradient wind on a non-frontal evening does not produce the regular 10–20° oscillations of a mature sea breeze. Expect a steady mean direction with gust-cycle variation. The slow overall rightward veer (SE → SSE over the window) makes the left-side geometry advantage grow slightly as the evening progresses — by 21:00 the SSE direction casts even less shadow from Greenwich Point than the earlier SE did. Commit to the geographic pressure lane and don't shift-hunt.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 stale (last reading 76 min ago) — over-water wind reference uses KBDR and RYC instead
HRRR/obs direction divergence: model shows ESE (114–118°) for 17:00–18:00, stations already at SE–SSE (145–156°); early-window direction uses RYC obs (SE 145°)
EW9544 APRS (Old Greenwich waterfront) flagged as outlier (temperature anomaly −5.1°F from median) — wind direction treated as informational
